entries.title: Week 11 Reflection: When Courts Don't Defer — Correctness Review and the Limits of Vavilov
author: Fukun

rich_text.text: Admin Law | Week of March 24, 2026
This week the course returned to the
Vavilov
framework to examine its limits. The presumption of reasonableness review — which we covered in Week 10 — is just that: a presumption.
Vavilov

carved out four categories where correctness applies instead: constitutional questions, questions of central importance to the legal system outside the adjudicator's expertise, questions regarding jurisdictional boundaries between administrative bodies, and cases with a legislated standard of review. The most practically significant of these is the first. When a decision raises a genuine constitutional question — whether the decision-maker had authority to act, whether a statute conforms to the
Constitution Act

, whether a Charter right was engaged — courts do not defer to the decision-maker's own view. They apply their own judgment, because the question of constitutional validity cannot, by its nature, be left to the institution whose authority is in question. The week also covered
Doré v Barreau du Québec
(2012 SCC 12), which governs how Charter rights are treated when they are engaged not by a statute but by a discretionary administrative decision — and
York Region District School Board v Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario
(2024 SCC 22), which clarified that the threshold question of whether a Charter right is engaged attracts correctness, while the proportionate balancing that follows is reviewed for reasonableness.
In February 2026, the Federal Court of Appeal released
Canada (Attorney General) v Canadian Civil Liberties Association
, 2026 FCA 6. The case arose from the federal government's invocation of the
Emergencies Act

in February 2022, during the Freedom Convoy protests in Ottawa. The Federal Court had already found in 2024 that the invocation was unlawful — the statutory threshold conditions for declaring a public order emergency had not been satisfied. The federal government appealed, arguing among other things that Cabinet decisions of this nature are not reviewable by federal courts at all. The FCA rejected that argument directly: Cabinet exercises statutory powers vested by legislation, and its decisions are accordingly subject to judicial review. There is no category of Cabinet decision that is simply immune from review.
Applying the
Vavilov
framework, the legal analysis is relatively clean. The question of whether the
Emergencies Act

invocation satisfied the constitutional and statutory conditions for its exercise is a constitutional and statutory scope question — it attracts correctness review, not reasonableness. Courts do not defer to a decision-maker's view on whether it had legal authority to act in the first place. That is precisely what the correctness exception for constitutional questions is designed to address. The government's argument for non-reviewability, if accepted, would have created a constitutional accountability gap — a domain of executive action insulated from independent judicial oversight — that conflicts not only with
Vavilov
but with the deeper principle from
Crevier

that jurisdictional and constitutional questions must remain reviewable. The FCA's answer reaffirmed that the scale of the decision, or the seniority of the decision-maker, does not change the basic constitutional structure.
The
York Region
case adds a further layer that is relevant if the
Emergencies Act

invocation also engaged Charter rights. Protesters argued that restrictions on the convoy engaged their s.2 rights (freedom of expression and peaceful assembly) and potentially s.7 (security of the person). Under the
York Region
framework, the threshold question — whether a Charter right was engaged at all — is a correctness question. But once engagement is established,
Doré

takes over: the decision-maker must demonstrate that it proportionately balanced the Charter right against the statutory objective it was pursuing. That balancing is reviewed for reasonableness, not correctness. This two-stage structure matters because it determines how much deference a court owes to the government's judgment about the balance struck between emergency powers and protected rights. On the threshold question — engagement — no deference. On the proportionate weighing — deference to reasonable balancing.
The
Emergencies Act

litigation is the most significant public law case in recent Canadian history, and it illustrates exactly what correctness review exists to do. Emergency powers, by their nature, operate quickly and broadly, affecting large numbers of people with limited prior consultation. They are also the powers most likely to be misused, because they are invoked in conditions of political pressure and public urgency. The
Vavilov

correctness exception for constitutional questions ensures that when a government reaches for that kind of authority, a court will independently verify that the constitutional conditions for doing so were actually met — not accept the government's own assessment. That independent check is not hostile to administrative expertise; it is the constitutional backstop that makes broad statutory delegation of power legitimate in the first place.

Cases referenced: Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65 | Doré v Barreau du Québec, 2012 SCC 12 | York Region District School Board v Elementary Teachers' Federation of Ontario, 2024 SCC 22 | Canada (Attorney General) v Canadian Civil Liberties Association, 2026 FCA 6 | Crevier v AG Quebec, [1981] 2 SCR 220
Sources
Canada (Attorney General) v Canadian Civil Liberties Association, 2026 FCA 6 — Analysis (Administrative Law Matters)
York Region District School Board v ETFO, 2024 SCC 22 — New Correctness Category (Harper Grey)
Doré v Barreau du Québec, 2012 SCC 12 (CanLII)
Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65 (CanLII)
A floodgate of correctness? New correctness categories post-Vavilov (Dentons)

entries.title: Week 10 Reflection: The HRTO Got Its Reasoning Wrong — and the Court Said So
author: Fukun

rich_text.text: Admin Law | Week of March 17, 2026

This week the course covered standard of review — the framework courts use to decide how closely to scrutinize an administrative decision when it is challenged on judicial review. The organizing case is
Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov
, 2019 SCC 65, which restructured the law by establishing a presumption of reasonableness review as the default, displacing the prior
Dunsmuir
framework's more contested category-sorting approach. Under
Vavilov

, reasonableness is not satisfied by any defensible outcome — a reasonable decision must be justified, transparent, and intelligible. It must engage with the key arguments the parties actually made and trace a logical path from the evidence and the law to the conclusion reached. Correctness review is reserved for a narrow set of categories: constitutional questions, questions of central importance to the legal system outside the adjudicator's expertise, and questions regarding jurisdictional boundaries between administrative bodies. For almost everything else, courts defer — but defer to the quality of the reasoning, not to the outcome alone.
On March 17 — the first class day of standard of review week — the Ontario Divisional Court released
Burland v Precise ParkLink Inc
, 2026 ONSC 1587. The Court quashed an
Human Rights Tribunal of Ontario

decision that had dismissed a human rights application. The problem was not that the HRTO reached the wrong result — the Divisional Court did not substitute its own view of the merits. The problem was that the Tribunal's reasons were "peremptory": brief, conclusory, and devoid of the justification, transparency, and intelligibility that
Vavilov
requires. The matter was remitted to be decided again, by a differently constituted panel, with reasons that actually engage with the applicant's case.
Applying the
Vavilov
framework to what went wrong in
Burland
makes the analysis concrete.
Vavilov

requires a reviewing court to read the decision as a whole, in context, and to ask whether the reasoning holds together — not whether it is perfect, but whether it is coherent and responsive to the live issues. Peremptory reasons fail this standard in a specific way: they tell the applicant that their complaint does not succeed, but they do not explain why. The applicant receives no understanding of which factual findings the Tribunal made, which legal test it applied, or which arguments it found unpersuasive. That is not just a procedural fairness problem — it is a substantive reasonableness problem, because a decision that cannot be traced from premises to conclusion cannot be assessed as reasonable or unreasonable by a reviewing court.
This is where the procedural fairness doctrine from Week 8 and the standard of review doctrine from this week reinforce each other rather than operating independently.
Baker
established a context-sensitive duty to give reasons where the stakes for the individual are significant.
Vavilov

made the quality of those reasons the central criterion of substantive reasonableness. Together, they create a layered accountability structure: a decision-maker must give reasons (procedural fairness), and those reasons must actually justify the decision (substantive reasonableness). A decision that technically produces a reasons document but fills it with bare conclusions satisfies neither requirement.
The
Vavilov
framework is sometimes described as a deferential standard that keeps courts out of administrative decision-making.
Burland
illustrates that this description is incomplete.
Vavilov

defers to the decision-maker's expertise and mandate — it does not defer to inadequate reasoning. The more specialized and expert the tribunal, the more its substantive judgments should be respected; but expert institutions must
show their work
. An HRTO that dismisses a human rights complaint without explaining why is not being deferred to under
Vavilov
— it is being sent back to try again.
Cases referenced: Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65 | Burland v Precise ParkLink Inc, 2026 ONSC 1587
Sources
Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65 (CanLII)
Human Rights Tribunal of Ontario
HRTO 2025 Case Law in Review — Ontario Bar Association

entries.title: Week 9 Reflection:  Does Zoom Create a Reasonable Apprehension of Bias
author: Fukun

rich_text.text: Admin Law | Week of March 10, 2026

This week the course covered the rule against bias — one of the oldest principles in natural justice. The doctrine draws two distinctions: actual bias, meaning a decision-maker who is genuinely partial, and reasonable apprehension of bias, which arises when the circumstances viewed by a well-informed observer raise a legitimate concern about impartiality even if the decision-maker is in fact neutral. The operative test comes from
Baker v Canada
[1999] 2 SCR 817 para 46, citing
Committee for Justice and Liberty v National Energy Board

[1978] 1 SCR 369: would an informed person, viewing the matter realistically and practically and having thought it through, have a reasonable apprehension that the decision-maker would not decide fairly? The test is objective — it asks about the appearance of the process to a hypothetical reasonable observer, not about the adjudicator's subjective state. A finding of reasonable apprehension of bias typically results in the decision being set aside regardless of whether the outcome was correct.

Since 2020, most Ontario administrative tribunals have moved to remote hearings and have largely stayed there. The LTB, LAT, HRTO, and Consent and Capacity Board all conduct a significant proportion of their hearings over Zoom.
Tribunals Ontario's remote hearings framework

describes this as an access improvement — no travel, no time off work, no childcare logistics. Disability rights advocates have broadly supported remote access. But reading this week's material on bias alongside the reality of how administrative adjudication now works raised a question the doctrine has not fully addressed: does a Zoom hearing change the
appearance
of impartiality in ways that could, in some circumstances, satisfy the reasonable apprehension test?
Applying the
Baker

para 46 test to a remote hearing requires thinking carefully about what the "informed observer" is actually watching. In a physical hearing room, signals of engagement or disengagement are visible to all parties: an adjudicator who is looking out the window, who leans forward when one party speaks but visibly disengages when the other does, or who makes impatient comments during one side's submissions sends observable signals. On Zoom, some of those signals are hidden — a camera-off adjudicator is simply invisible — and others are amplified or displaced into different channels. Pre-hearing written communications in case management platforms, for example, can document preliminary views in ways that in-person case management never would have. An adjudicator who records a view on the merits before hearing submissions, in writing, creates a discoverable record that might not have existed before.
The conclusion the
Baker

test supports is that Zoom does not automatically create a reasonable apprehension of bias, but specific circumstances in a remote hearing can satisfy the test just as they would in person. A camera-off adjudicator who engages actively through questions, who distributes hearing time fairly, and who gives no other adverse signal probably does not create a reasonable apprehension — the informed observer would see a process that, however impersonal, appears impartial. But an adjudicator whose written pre-hearing notes express a preliminary view on the merits of one party's position, or whose audio reveals active engagement during one party's submissions and silence during the other's, may well cross the threshold. The test is about appearance, not modality. What
Zoom
changes is the set of observable signals available to the informed observer — which means the same analysis applies, but through a different lens.
The harder institutional question is whether remote hearings as a
system

affect the perceived legitimacy of adjudication in ways the individual bias test doesn't capture. The physical hearing room carries social meaning that Zoom does not replicate — the shared public space, the visible formality of the process, the experience of being heard before a decision-maker who is present in the same room. Whether the loss of that social dimension constitutes a fairness problem that the bias doctrine should address, or whether it is simply a change in form that procedural fairness law is not designed to regulate, is a question I don't think the existing case law has resolved.
Cases referenced: Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817 | Committee for Justice and Liberty v National Energy Board, [1978] 1 SCR 369
Sources
Tribunals Ontario — Remote Hearings
Baker v Canada, 1999 SCC 40 (CanLII)

entries.title: Week 8: How Did We Get a Duty of Fairness, and What Does It Now Require?
author: Fukun

rich_text.text: Admin Law | Week of March 3, 2026

This week traced the evolution of procedural fairness in administrative law across more than 150 years of cases. The story is one of expansion: from a narrow common law principle limited to "judicial" functions, through a series of SCC decisions that dismantled the category-based approach and replaced it with a contextual interest-based standard, to
Baker v Canada
[1999] 2 SCR 817's five-factor synthesis, and finally to
Law Society of Saskatchewan v Abrametz

(2022 SCC 29), which extended the fairness inquiry to the duration of proceedings. The central lesson was how the doctrine moved from asking "what type of function is this?" to asking "what is at stake for this person, and what process does that demand?"
Abrametz

is both course material and a concrete recent decision worth understanding precisely. Peter Abrametz was a Saskatchewan lawyer found guilty of conduct unbecoming, including making high-interest loans to vulnerable clients. Disciplinary proceedings commenced in 2012 and concluded in 2019 — a 71-month span. The Saskatchewan Court of Appeal stayed his disbarment, finding the delay constituted an abuse of process. The
SCC reversed 8-1
: Justice Rowe, writing for the majority, held that the delay was long but not
inordinate
given the complexity of the matter and Abrametz's own contribution to the timeline.
Administrative law commentators
noted that the significance of the case lies not in the outcome for Abrametz — he lost — but in the two-part test the majority articulated: delay constitutes an abuse of process where it is
inordinate
in all the circumstances,
and
where it causes
significant prejudice
to the person subject to it. Both elements must be present; the threshold is demanding.
Understanding why
Abrametz
looks the way it does requires following the evolutionary arc the week covered. The starting point is
Cooper v Wandsworth Board of Works

(1863) 143 ER 414, where an English court held that even a statute silent on procedure did not eliminate the common law hearing right — "no man shall be condemned unheard." For a long time, courts confined this to "judicial" functions and refused to apply it to "administrative" ones, producing inconsistent results across provinces and decision-making bodies.
Ridge v Baldwin
[1964] AC 40 began the shift: Lord Reid held the right to a hearing depended on whether rights or interests were affected, not on whether the function was formally judicial. The SCC followed with
Nicholson v Haldimand-Norfolk Regional Police Commissioners
[1979] 1 SCR 311, which introduced a freestanding "duty of fairness" not dependent on classifying the function as quasi-judicial, and then
Martineau v Matsqui Institution
Cardinal v Director of Kent Institution
[1985] 2 SCR 643, which made it a general common law rule.
Knight v Indian Head School Division
[1990] 1 SCR 653 added that the
content
of the duty varies on a spectrum — minimal process for broad policy decisions affecting the public, more robust process for individualized decisions affecting named people's rights.
Baker

synthesized all of this into five factors: nature of the decision, statutory scheme, importance to the individual, legitimate expectations, and the agency's own procedural choices. The five factors do not produce a formula, but they give structure to the judgment. Applied to a serious AGCO licence revocation — a named individual, significant economic consequences, a statutory scheme that contemplates adjudication — the
Baker

factors would require meaningful notice, an opportunity to respond, and written reasons. Applied to a broad IRCC policy announcement affecting all international students in a given category, the duty is much thinner.
Abrametz

extended the inquiry further: it is not only the content of the hearing steps that counts, but the time in which they unfold. Delay that is inordinate and causes significant prejudice — reputational harm, professional uncertainty, prolonged anxiety — is itself a fairness violation. For international students navigating immigration proceedings that can span years, this matters. An immigration appeal, a refugee claim, or a permit refusal under judicial review can impose the same kinds of prejudice that Abrametz suffered: precarious status, inability to plan, restricted rights.
Abrametz

confirms that delay is a cognizable legal wrong, not just an administrative inconvenience — though the threshold of inordinate delay plus significant prejudice is high enough that most delays will not cross it. What the case establishes, at minimum, is that duration is a dimension of fairness the doctrine now formally recognizes.

Cases referenced: Law Society of Saskatchewan v Abrametz, 2022 SCC 29 | Cooper v Wandsworth Board of Works (1863) 143 ER 414 | Ridge v Baldwin, [1964] AC 40 | Nicholson v Haldimand-Norfolk Regional Police Commissioners, [1979] 1 SCR 311 | Martineau v Matsqui Institution, [1980] 1 SCR 602 | Cardinal v Director of Kent Institution, [1985] 2 SCR 643 | Knight v Indian Head School Division, [1990] 1 SCR 653 | Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817
Sources

Disciplinary proceedings commenced in 2012 and concluded in 2019 — a 71-month span. The Saskatchewan Court of Appeal stayed his disbarment, finding the delay constituted an abuse of process. The SCC reversed 8-1: Justice Rowe, writing for the majority, held that the delay was long but not inordinate given the complexity of the matter and Abrametz's own contribution to the timeline. Administrative law commentators noted that the significance of the case lies not in the outcome for Abrametz — he lost — but in the two-part test the majority articulated: delay constitutes an abuse of process where it is inordinate in all the circumstances, and where it causes significant prejudice to the person subject to it. Both elements must be present; the threshold is demanding.
Understanding why Abrametz looks the way it does requires following the evolutionary arc the week covered. The starting point is Cooper v Wandsworth Board of Works (1863) 143 ER 414, where an English court held that even a statute silent on procedure did not eliminate the common law hearing right — "no man shall be condemned unheard." For a long time, courts confined this to "judicial" functions and refused to apply it to "administrative" ones, producing inconsistent results across provinces and decision-making bodies. Ridge v Baldwin [1964] AC 40 began the shift: Lord Reid held the right to a hearing depended on whether rights or interests were affected, not on whether the function was formally judicial. The SCC followed with Nicholson v Haldimand-Norfolk Regional Police Commissioners [1979] 1 SCR 311, which introduced a freestanding "duty of fairness" not dependent on classifying the function as quasi-judicial, and then Martineau v Matsqui Institution [1980] 1 SCR 602 and Cardinal v Director of Kent Institution [1985] 2 SCR 643, which made it a general common law rule. Knight v Indian Head School Division [1990] 1 SCR 653 added that the content of the duty varies on a spectrum — minimal process for broad policy decisions affecting the public, more robust process for individualized decisions affecting named people's rights.
Baker synthesized all of this into five factors: nature of the decision, statutory scheme, importance to the individual, legitimate expectations, and the agency's own procedural choices. The five factors do not produce a formula, but they give structure to the judgment. Applied to a serious AGCO licence revocation — a named individual, significant economic consequences, a statutory scheme that contemplates adjudication — the Baker factors would require meaningful notice, an opportunity to respond, and written reasons. Applied to a broad IRCC policy announcement affecting all international students in a given category, the duty is much thinner.
Abrametz extended the inquiry further: it is not only the content of the hearing steps that counts, but the time in which they unfold. Delay that is inordinate and causes significant prejudice — reputational harm, professional uncertainty, prolonged anxiety — is itself a fairness violation. For international students navigating immigration proceedings that can span years, this matters. An immigration appeal, a refugee claim, or a permit refusal under judicial review can impose the same kinds of prejudice that Abrametz suffered: precarious status, inability to plan, restricted rights. Abrametz confirms that delay is a cognizable legal wrong, not just an administrative inconvenience — though the threshold of inordinate delay plus significant prejudice is high enough that most delays will not cross it. What the case establishes, at minimum, is that duration is a dimension of fairness the doctrine now formally recognizes.
Cases referenced: Law Society of Saskatchewan v Abrametz, 2022 SCC 29 | Cooper v Wandsworth Board of Works (1863) 143 ER 414 | Ridge v Baldwin, [1964] AC 40 | Nicholson v Haldimand-Norfolk Regional Police Commissioners, [1979] 1 SCR 311 | Martineau v Matsqui Institution, [1980] 1 SCR 602 | Cardinal v Director of Kent Institution, [1985] 2 SCR 643 | Knight v Indian Head School Division, [1990] 1 SCR 653 | Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817
---

entries.title: Week 7: Legitimate Expectations and the PGWP Policy Shift
author: Fukun

rich_text.text: Admin Law | Week of February 24, 2026

This week covered the doctrine of legitimate expectations — the principle that, in certain circumstances, a person who relies on a clear government representation has a legal entitlement to procedural protection before that representation is resiled from. Canadian law draws a firm line: legitimate expectations is a
procedural

doctrine only. A successful claim gives the applicant a right to a fair process — notice, an opportunity to be heard — before a policy changes. It does not guarantee any substantive outcome. The three central cases were
Mavi v Canada
(2011 SCC 30), which tied the doctrine to formal undertakings and confirmed the procedural-only limit;
Agraira v Canada
(2013 SCC 36), which extended it to published ministerial guidelines where sufficiently clear and consistent; and
Baker v Canada

[1999] 2 SCR 817, which emphasized that context and the degree of individual reliance matter in determining what procedural protection is owed. The doctrine's threshold — a representation that is clear, unambiguous, and unqualified — was the central point of discussion.
On September 18, 2024, IRCC
announced significant changes to Post-Graduation Work Permit eligibility
, introducing a new requirement that PGWP applicants' programs align with
specific labour market fields

— STEM, healthcare, agriculture, trades, and transportation. Hospitality, social services, and the arts were excluded. The transition protection was narrow: students who had applied for their initial study permit before November 1, 2024 were exempt; those already enrolled but who had not yet submitted that application were not.
As Green and Spiegel summarized
, students who had chosen specific programs precisely because IRCC's materials indicated those programs would qualify for a PGWP found themselves subject to rules they had no way to anticipate.
Protests followed across Canada

Applying the week's doctrine to these facts produces a concrete answer, even if it is not a satisfying one. The legitimate expectations analysis starts with the threshold requirement: was IRCC's published PGWP guidance a representation that was clear, unambiguous, and unqualified? The answer is almost certainly no. IRCC's web publications on PGWP eligibility have consistently included qualifications — rules are subject to change, programs must continue to meet requirements, no binding commitment is made. Under
Mavi
, the doctrine requires something closer to a formal undertaking made to an identified party with specific legal content. A general eligibility page with caveats does not meet that standard. Under
Agraira

, a published guideline might generate expectations if it is sufficiently clear and consistently applied — but the caveats in IRCC's publications were precisely designed to preserve the government's flexibility to change the rules, which takes the PGWP materials below the
Agraira
threshold.

Even if the threshold were met, the procedural-only limit would significantly constrain what students could obtain. A successful legitimate expectations claim would have required IRCC to give affected students notice of the proposed change and a meaningful opportunity to comment before it took effect — not a right to have the old rules applied to their applications. If IRCC had conducted that process and then still adopted the new field-of-study requirement, the legal outcome for students would be identical. The doctrine gives access to process, not to a result.

The honest conclusion is that the legitimate expectations doctrine, as currently defined in Canadian law, provides limited protection against the kind of harm the 2024 PGWP changes caused. Students who structured significant financial and immigration decisions around government-published eligibility information received no legally enforceable procedural protection when those rules changed. The gap between the harm experienced and the legal remedy available reflects a deliberate doctrinal choice — to preserve government flexibility to change policy — rather than an oversight. Whether that balance is correctly struck is a policy question the courts have consistently declined to revisit.

Cases referenced: Mavi v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 2011 SCC 30 | Agraira v Canada (Public Safety and Emergency Preparedness), 2013 SCC 36 | Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817
Sources

Post-graduation work permit: Field of study requirement (
Canada.ca
What You Need to Know About the New PGWP Requirements (Green and Spiegel)
Indian students protest PGWP rules in Canada (The PIE News)

entries.title: Week 5: Did the LTB Actually Survive s. 96, or Just Pass a Low Bar?
author: Fukun

rich_text.text: Admin Law | Week of February 10, 2026

This week covered one of the more unusual constitutional questions in Canadian law: can a provincial legislature take a type of dispute that courts historically decided and hand it to an administrative tribunal instead? Section 96 of the
Constitution Act, 1867

gives the federal government the power to appoint judges of superior courts. The SCC has interpreted this to mean that provinces cannot simply strip superior courts of their core jurisdiction and transfer it to a tribunal that the province controls — that would let provinces hollow out federally-appointed courts by delegating their work elsewhere. So when Ontario created the Landlord and Tenant Board to handle evictions and tenancy disputes, there was a genuine constitutional question: was the province doing something it was constitutionally allowed to do?
The answer depends on a three-step test developed in
Reference re Residential Tenancies Act, 1979

(Ontario) [1981] 1 SCR 714. First, characterize the power the tribunal is being given. Second, ask whether that power looks like something superior courts exclusively or primarily handled at the time of Confederation in 1867. Third, even if it does, ask whether the tribunal is embedded in a broader regulatory scheme that is distinctly modern — something that wasn't just reproducing what courts used to do, but administering a new kind of statutory regime. The 1981 Ontario scheme failed at step three: the tribunal's power to evict tenants looked too much like what courts had always done, and there was no administrative scheme substantial enough to distinguish it. The legislature had to go back and redesign. The modern LTB, created under the
Residential Tenancies Act, 2006

, passed all three steps because the new scheme did far more than reproduce common law eviction rules — it created new statutory rights for tenants that had never existed at common law, like the right to maintenance, the right to dispute rent increases, and the right to be free from illegal lockouts. The
Reference re Residential Tenancies Act
(Nova Scotia) [1996] 1 SCR 186 confirmed the same approach worked for a comparable scheme in another province.
In May 2023, Ontario's Ombudsman released
Administrative Justice Delayed, Fairness Denied

, a three-year investigation into the LTB. The backlog stood at more than 38,000 cases. Median wait times were seven to eight months for landlord applications; some tenant applications took up to two years.
CBC News described the board as "fundamentally failing."
The
Advocacy Centre for Tenants Ontario
documented cases of tenants stuck in units with mould, broken heating, and pest infestations while waiting months for a maintenance hearing.
Tribunal Watch Ontario
tracked the numbers quarter by quarter. This is where the constitutional framework's limits become visible, because none of what the Ombudsman documented was a constitutional problem.

The three-step test asks one specific question: should this type of dispute be decided by a tribunal at all, or does it belong exclusively to the courts? Once the LTB cleared that test — and it did — the constitution had nothing more to say about whether the LTB needed more adjudicators, faster scheduling, or better technology. Those are resource and policy questions, which are the legislature's to answer. A tenant waiting eight months for a hearing about a mould problem in their apartment is accessing a constitutionally valid institution. The constitution validated the institution's right to exist. It said nothing about the institution's obligation to actually function.
Two additional cases bracket what oversight remains.
Crevier v AG Quebec

[1981] 2 SCR 220 ensures that if the LTB makes a decision that falls outside its statutory authority — deciding something it was never empowered to decide — superior courts can step in and correct it, no matter what the statute says.
MacMillan Bloedel Ltd v Simpson

[1995] 4 SCR 725 ensures that the legislature cannot take away superior court jurisdiction entirely; courts always retain an irreducible supervisory role. Both cases protect against the LTB overreaching its mandate. Neither requires the LTB to process its current caseload efficiently. For international students in Ontario who rent — often in older housing stock, often unfamiliar with the LTB's procedures, and sometimes reluctant to make complaints for fear of disrupting their housing — the gap between what the constitution guarantees and what the tribunal actually delivers is the part that matters most in practice.

Cases referenced: Reference re Residential Tenancies Act, 1979 (Ontario), [1981] 1 SCR 714 | Reference re Residential Tenancies Act (Nova Scotia), [1996] 1 SCR 186 | Crevier v AG Quebec, [1981] 2 SCR 220 | MacMillan Bloedel Ltd v Simpson, [1995] 4 SCR 725
Sources
Administrative Justice Delayed, Fairness Denied — Ombudsman Ontario (May 2023)
Ontario's Landlord and Tenant Board 'fundamentally failing,' ombudsman finds (CBC News)
The Landlord and Tenant Board is broken (Advocacy Centre for Tenants Ontario)
LTB Statement of Concern: The Numbers Speak for Themselves (Tribunal Watch Ontario)

entries.title: Week 4: When Does a Guideline Become Illegal Control?
author: Fukun

rich_text.text: Admin Law | Week of February 3, 2026

This week covered soft law — policies, guidelines, and internal instructions that agencies use to structure discretion without formal rule-making — and introduced the fettering doctrine that governs when those tools cross a legal line. The key tension runs between two cases.
Maple Lodge Farms Ltd v Canada

[1982] 2 SCR 2 established that mechanically applying a policy without genuine consideration of the individual case is not a lawful exercise of discretion — it is a substitution of the policy for the judgment Parliament intended. But
Baker v Canada
[1999] 2 SCR 817 treated ministerial guidelines as legitimate and important context for structuring discretion, and
Agraira v Canada

(2013 SCC 36) confirmed that a reasonable officer would take published guidelines seriously. The doctrinal message is that guidelines are necessary and lawful as a framework, but cannot function as a checklist that replaces case-by-case judgment.

That tension maps directly onto something immigration lawyers have raised publicly for years. In 2022 and 2023, concerns were documented about how IRCC officers assessed the "genuineness of studies" requirement for study permit applications. The operative criteria come from internal
Program Delivery Instructions
— documents that are not published as regulations and not subject to parliamentary oversight. The
International Bar Association has noted
that "genuineness" refusals are among the most common and hardest to challenge, often arriving as boilerplate with no applicant-specific reasoning. Lawyers have used
access-to-information requests and procedural fairness letter processes

to reconstruct which criteria were actually applied — sometimes finding that officers appeared to be working through a checklist of indicators (travel history, financial ties, family circumstances) rather than engaging with the individual file.
This is the
Maple Lodge Farms
problem applied to immigration. The PDI provides a structured framework; the officer works through it; a refusal is issued. The legal question is whether the officer used the framework as a
tool for judgment
or as a
substitute for judgment

. A refusal that identifies that the applicant "has not demonstrated sufficient ties to their home country" without connecting that finding to any specific evidence in the file looks more like the latter. The challenge, which the cases do not fully resolve, is that from the outside a thoughtful application of the framework and a mechanical one can produce identical output. Both cite the same criteria; both arrive at the same result. The fettering problem is partly an evidentiary one.
Vavilov
(2019 SCC 65) is where the doctrine provides a practical mechanism. Reasonableness review under
Vavilov

requires decision-makers to produce reasons that actually grapple with the key considerations in the record — including a demonstration that the guidelines were applied to the specific applicant's circumstances, not just cited. A refusal letter that reproduces PDI language without showing how the officer assessed the particular evidence is more vulnerable to a finding of unreasonableness than one that engages with the file.
Vavilov
does not eliminate fettering, but it makes it harder to sustain on review by requiring visible reasoning.

The gap that remains is upstream of the individual decision. The PDIs themselves are not easily accessible to applicants. If you don't know the operative framework, you cannot structure your application to address its criteria, and you cannot identify on review whether those criteria were applied thoughtfully. The fettering doctrine addresses the quality of the decision made; the opacity of the framework that shaped it is a separate problem — one that
Vavilov
and
Baker
together do not fully answer.

Cases referenced: Maple Lodge Farms Ltd v Canada, [1982] 2 SCR 2 | Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817 | Agraira v Canada (Public Safety and Emergency Preparedness), 2013 SCC 36 | Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65
Sources
Study permit refusals in Canada (International Bar Association)
IRCC Procedural Fairness Letter — How to Respond (Canadian Currents Immigration)
Study Permit Application Refusals (Canadavisa)
IRCC Ministerial Instructions (
Canada.ca

entries.title: Week 3: Does Institutional Design Actually Matter?
author: Fukun

rich_text.text: Admin Law | Week of January 27, 2026
This week the course covered the constitutional framework governing how administrative tribunals are designed. Three cases define the boundaries.
Ocean Port Hotel Ltd v BC

(2001 SCC 52) held that structural features like member tenure and independence are not constitutionally required — they are legislative choices, and courts will not impose their own design preferences on Parliament or the provinces.
Crevier v AG Quebec
[1981] 2 SCR 220 set the constitutional floor: whatever the legislature designs, it cannot immunize a tribunal's jurisdictional errors from superior court review.
MacMillan Bloedel Ltd v Simpson

[1995] 4 SCR 725 established the outer hard limit: the irreducible core of superior court jurisdiction cannot be fully abolished. Together, these three cases map a spectrum of legislative freedom — wide design latitude under
Ocean Port
, a jurisdictional floor under
Crevier
, and an outer constitutional stop under
MacMillan Bloedel
In December 2021, the AGCO
suspended and moved to revoke the liquor licence of Zucchinis Cucina
, an Oakville restaurant that had admitted unvaccinated dine-in patrons during Ontario's proof-of-vaccination period. It was part of a broader enforcement campaign — the AGCO's
COVID-19 pandemic response

describes Compliance Officials participating in multi-ministry enforcement sweeps. Some operators complained about the speed of the actions and argued that the AGCO's published progressive enforcement framework was not being consistently followed. The right way to assess those complaints is to run them through the week's constitutional framework.
Start with
Ocean Port
. The AGCO's structure — a governing Commission setting policy, a Registrar applying it to individual cases, and a separate Licence Appeal Tribunal reviewing challenges — is entirely a product of the
Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario Act, 2019
. Under
Ocean Port

, all of that is a valid legislative choice. The degree of Commission oversight over the Registrar, the partial separation between policy-setting and adjudication, the tenure conditions of LAT members — these are design decisions the legislature was entitled to make, and a court applying
Ocean Port
will not second-guess them. An operator who thinks the AGCO is structurally biased has no constitutional argument based on design alone.
Next,
Crevier
. The jurisdictional floor matters if the Registrar's enforcement exceeded its statutory authority — for example, if it suspended a licence on grounds that the
AGCO Act

does not actually authorize. If the Registrar acted outside its statutory remit, the LAT and, beyond it, superior courts retain jurisdiction to review that excess, and no privative clause can take that away. On the COVID enforcement facts, the statutory authority question turns on whether violations of public health orders issued under separate legislation were valid grounds for AGCO action under the
AGCO Act
. That is a genuine legal question, but the AGCO does not appear to have faced a successful jurisdictional challenge on this basis, suggesting courts found the statutory authority was present.
The conclusion from applying all three cases to the Zucchinis Cucina scenario is that the AGCO's design and its COVID enforcement actions were constitutionally sound.
Ocean Port
protected the design;
Crevier
preserved judicial oversight;
MacMillan Bloedel

entries.body:
Admin Law | Week of January 27, 2026
This week the course covered the constitutional framework governing how administrative tribunals are designed. Three cases define the boundaries. Ocean Port Hotel Ltd v BC (2001 SCC 52) held that structural features like member tenure and independence are not constitutionally required — they are legislative choices, and courts will not impose their own design preferences on Parliament or the provinces. Crevier v AG Quebec [1981] 2 SCR 220 set the constitutional floor: whatever the legislature designs, it cannot immunize a tribunal's jurisdictional errors from superior court review. MacMillan Bloedel Ltd v Simpson [1995] 4 SCR 725 established the outer hard limit: the irreducible core of superior court jurisdiction cannot be fully abolished. Together, these three cases map a spectrum of legislative freedom — wide design latitude under Ocean Port, a jurisdictional floor under Crevier, and an outer constitutional stop under MacMillan Bloedel.
In December 2021, the AGCO suspended and moved to revoke the liquor licence of Zucchinis Cucina, an Oakville restaurant that had admitted unvaccinated dine-in patrons during Ontario's proof-of-vaccination period. It was part of a broader enforcement campaign — the AGCO's COVID-19 pandemic response describes Compliance Officials participating in multi-ministry enforcement sweeps. Some operators complained about the speed of the actions and argued that the AGCO's published progressive enforcement framework was not being consistently followed. The right way to assess those complaints is to run them through the week's constitutional framework.
Start with Ocean Port. The AGCO's structure — a governing Commission setting policy, a Registrar applying it to individual cases, and a separate Licence Appeal Tribunal reviewing challenges — is entirely a product of the Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario Act, 2019. Under Ocean Port, all of that is a valid legislative choice. The degree of Commission oversight over the Registrar, the partial separation between policy-setting and adjudication, the tenure conditions of LAT members — these are design decisions the legislature was entitled to make, and a court applying Ocean Port will not second-guess them. An operator who thinks the AGCO is structurally biased has no constitutional argument based on design alone.
Next, Crevier. The jurisdictional floor matters if the Registrar's enforcement exceeded its statutory authority — for example, if it suspended a licence on grounds that the AGCO Act does not actually authorize. If the Registrar acted outside its statutory remit, the LAT and, beyond it, superior courts retain jurisdiction to review that excess, and no privative clause can take that away. On the COVID enforcement facts, the statutory authority question turns on whether violations of public health orders issued under separate legislation were valid grounds for AGCO action under the AGCO Act. That is a genuine legal question, but the AGCO does not appear to have faced a successful jurisdictional challenge on this basis, suggesting courts found the statutory authority was present.
The conclusion from applying all three cases to the Zucchinis Cucina scenario is that the AGCO's design and its COVID enforcement actions were constitutionally sound. Ocean Port protected the design; Crevier preserved judicial oversight; MacMillan Bloedel's outer limit was nowhere near triggered. But the more important takeaway is that constitutional validity does not exhaust the legal inquiry. Law Society of Ontario v AA (2026 ONCA 47), decided just recently, shows that the ONCA will separately ask whether the process in a specific case was adequate to the stakes involved — a Baker-style procedural fairness question that operates entirely independently of whether the tribunal's structure is constitutionally valid. For AGCO operators who received little notice before a suspension, that procedural fairness question is the relevant one, and it is where the real legal dispute lives.
Cases referenced: Ocean Port Hotel Ltd v British Columbia, 2001 SCC 52 | Crevier v AG Quebec, [1981] 2 SCR 220 | MacMillan Bloedel Ltd v Simpson, [1995] 4 SCR 725 | Law Society of Ontario v AA, 2026 ONCA 47

entries.title: Week 2: What Makes a Decision "Governmental Enough" to Review?
author: Fukun

rich_text.text: Admin Law | Week of January 20, 2026

This week built on last week's question — not just what an administrative decision-maker is, but which ones are actually subject to judicial review. Judicial review is the process by which courts check whether a public body acted within its legal authority and treated people fairly. It is a powerful tool, but it is not available against every organization that makes decisions affecting people's lives. The
Wall
test (2018 SCC 26) determines when it applies: a body must be exercising
public

power — authority that flows from the state through legislation and that makes the body governmental in nature. If the body's authority comes from a contract, from consent, or from a purely private arrangement, the courts say: that is a private matter, and private law — not judicial review — is your remedy.

During the COVID-19 pandemic, Ontario condominium corporations issued rules restricting elevator access, banning guests, and in some buildings requiring proof of vaccination just to use shared amenities. Some residents received fines.
CBC News reported
on a downtown Toronto condo rolling out a vaccine policy for common areas; the
Globe and Mail noted

that boards were left to set their own rules without provincial guidance. A fined resident asking "can I challenge this in court?" would quickly run into the Wall test. Condo boards get their governance powers from the
Condominium Act
, but the nature of the relationship is fundamentally private — you bought into a community of property owners, and disputes within that community stay there.
Courts treated COVID condo policies as valid exercises of board authority
, enforceable through the Condominium Authority Tribunal and the Act's own dispute resolution processes, not through judicial review. The
Wall
test gave the right answer here.
Before
Wall

, courts had tried three different approaches to drawing this line, and each produced different results for the same kinds of bodies. One approach focused on where the legal authority came from — does it trace back to a statute? Another looked at a list of factors — government funding, government control, the public character of the function — to determine whether the body was "sufficiently governmental." A third tried to distinguish between bodies exercising statutory powers and bodies exercising private powers even within a regulated industry. The problem was that condo boards, universities, and professional associations could come out differently depending on which approach a judge preferred.
Wall
replaced all three with one question: is the power the body is exercising a
public
power? That produced more predictable outcomes, even if the boundary remains genuinely difficult in some cases.
Aga v Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church of Canada St. Mary Cathedral
(2021 SCC 22), decided three years after
Wall
, shows where the clean answer gets uncomfortable. A religious community expelled members from the church. The SCC applied
Wall

and held the decision was not reviewable — the church's authority over its members comes from religious community and consent, not from the state. Legally, this is correct. Practically, it means that people expelled from a tight-knit religious community, with serious social and personal consequences, have no public law remedy at all. The
Wall
test delivered clarity; it did not promise justice for every situation it classifies as private.

For me as an international student, the most consequential institution in my daily life is not a government agency — it is my university. Universities are created by provincial statute and receive public funding, but they also have a private, consensual dimension: you chose to enroll, you agreed to the institution's rules. A finding of academic dishonesty can cost me my enrolment and, because my study permit is tied to being a full-time student, can unravel my immigration status in Canada. Whether that disciplinary decision is subject to judicial review or left to the university's internal process depends on exactly the
Wall
characterization.
MacKinnon v University of Guelph
(2025 FC 422) shows federal courts are actively grappling with where universities sit.
Wall

simplified the test. It did not resolve the cases where the line between public and private is genuinely uncertain — and for students in serious disciplinary proceedings, uncertainty about which forum applies is itself a serious problem.

Cases referenced: Wall v Independent Planning Association, 2018 SCC 26 | Aga v Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church of Canada St. Mary Cathedral, 2021 SCC 22 | MacKinnon v University of Guelph, 2025 FC 422
Sources
Toronto condo building brings in vaccine policy for amenities (CBC News)
In condo land, COVID-19 vaccine rules are hazy (The Globe and Mail)
Condo COVID-19 Policy Upheld as Enforceable by Court (Lash Condo Law)

entries.title: Week 1: Who Is Actually Making the Decision?
author: Fukun

rich_text.text: Admin Law | Week of January 13, 2026

This week introduced the foundational question of administrative law: what makes a body an "administrative decision-maker" subject to judicial review? The answer the course gave is the source-of-power test — a body exercises public law power when its authority derives from statute, and it is that statutory delegation that triggers administrative law accountability.
Roncarelli v Duplessis

[1959] SCR 121 is the classic illustration: when a government official used statutory power to revoke a liquor licence as personal retaliation, the SCC held that the exercise of that authority was legally constrained precisely because the power was public. Statutory source equals public accountability.
When I renewed my study permit last year, the first step was not submitting anything to IRCC — it was booking an appointment through
VFS Global

, a private company contracted by the government to collect biometrics and application documents. You pay a separate service fee to VFS, attend their commercial office, and only then does IRCC receive your materials and make the actual decision. IRCC's own
VFS consent form

states explicitly: "VFS Global is not an agent of the Government of Canada but is a completely independent entity operating under contract." That sentence matters legally. If something goes wrong at the VFS stage — documents mishandled, biometrics incorrectly recorded — the source-of-power test points to a clear answer: VFS has no statutory authority, it is not an administrative decision-maker, and I have no administrative law remedy against it.
A similar pattern appears with ServiceOntario, which has contracted out vehicle and driver licensing services to private registry agents — pharmacies, private offices, dealerships — for years. The
Auditor General of Ontario

has flagged the accountability gap this creates: when private operators make errors affecting people's licences, there is no clear public law path to correction because the operators are not statutory decision-makers. These are not hypothetical problems. They affect real people whose licences, permits, and status depend on functions that the government has delegated to private hands.

Applying the source-of-power test to VFS produces a technically correct but practically unsatisfying result. VFS receives documents; IRCC makes the eligibility decision. The statutory power to grant or refuse a study permit lives with IRCC under the
Immigration and Refugee Protection Act

. VFS exercises no delegated public law power and is therefore, on this analysis, outside administrative law's reach. What this conclusion misses is that VFS is a mandatory gateway to a statutory right — there is no way to submit a study permit application without going through VFS for biometrics. An error at that stage can delay or derail an application with serious downstream consequences for immigration status, employment authorization, and study continuity. The person harmed has no direct administrative law remedy against the body that caused the harm.
The source-of-power test was developed when government acted through its own officials, not through contracted private infrastructure. The
Roncarelli

principle — that public authority creates public accountability — has not yet been applied to ask who is accountable when public authority is intentionally routed through private architecture. Until the doctrine catches up with the reality of outsourced service delivery, there is a genuine accountability gap that affects people who have no way to avoid it.
Cases referenced: Roncarelli v Duplessis, [1959] SCR 121
Sources
Biometrics — Where to give your fingerprints and photo (IRCC)
VFS Global Consent Form for Canadian Visa Applications
Office of the Auditor General of Ontario
